policy_statement: Resource community-led climate infrastructure projects by 2022.
mechanism_description: Providing resources for community-led climate infrastructure projects enables deployment of mitigation and adaptation measures, supporting emissions reductions and improved climate resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Public investment/funding → deployment of resilience infrastructure/services → ↓ hazard exposure/↓ vulnerability → ↓ climate damages
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human/system activity: Government/municipality provides funding, grants, or resourcing to community-led climate infrastructure projects (planning, capital works, retrofits, preparedness assets).  
2) Mediating physical/process link: Funded projects are built/implemented, improving protective capacity (e.g., stormwater management, cooling access, backup power, hardened facilities), preparedness, and response capability.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced impacts from climate hazards (heat, flooding, storms, etc.) through lowered exposure and vulnerability; any GHG reductions are indirect and depend on project types funded, so they are not inherent to the mechanism.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is enabling deployment of resilience measures that reduce exposure/vulnerability to climate hazards by funding climate infrastructure projects.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s direct, universal function is to finance/enable implementation of climate infrastructure, which most consistently yields resilience outcomes by reducing hazard impacts.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “climate infrastructure” can include mitigation (e.g., efficiency, electrification), emissions reductions are not guaranteed across all funded-project portfolios; resilience benefits (reduced risk via protective/adaptive infrastructure) are the most robust cross-instance effect, especially given the explicit “community resiliency” framing in the provided policy.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—mitigation/resource-efficiency benefits occur only if the funded projects specifically target energy/GHG outcomes, which is not unavoidable.  
4) Edge cases applied: If a jurisdiction defines the funding program narrowly for GHG-reducing infrastructure only (e.g., EV charging, building electrification), the dominant pathway would shift to Mitigation; this is not specified in the mechanism as given.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any instance of “resourcing climate infrastructure projects” will at minimum enable adaptive/resilience measures; mitigation is optional and therefore not assigned as inherent secondary.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the funding program is explicitly restricted to decarbonization infrastructure (renewables, electrification, efficiency) or has binding emissions targets as the primary purpose, classification should switch to Mitigation; if it is restricted to green infrastructure/ecosystem restoration, it may instead be Nature-Based Solutions.
co_benefits: Potential local economic development/community capacity-building benefits from funding community-led projects; potential mitigation co-benefits depending on project types.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Resource” is ambiguous between direct capital spending vs. a grant/incentive program; classified as infrastructure-investment because the object is “infrastructure projects,” but it could alternatively be framed as an incentive-program if implemented primarily via grants.
